metadata.seed: I work and work and work but I can't seem to get ahead. The volume at work has gotten so bad that I can't keep up. I'm getting injured and no one cares. I can barely pay my bills and it feels like there is no end in sight.

patient.query: I work and work and work but I can't seem to get ahead. The volume at work has gotten so bad that I can't keep up. I'm getting injured and no one cares. I can barely pay my bills and it feels like there is no end in sight.

automatic_thoughts: I can't get ahead
no one cares
there is no end in sight

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling overwhelmed and stuck. What moments, if any, have you noticed where you were able to manage the workload or make progress? How might you reframe the idea that no one cares or that there’s no end in sight?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like a lot right now. I guess there were times when I managed to push through and get things done, especially when I focused on just one small thing at a time. But then those thoughts creep back in, you know? Like, will it ever really get better? It’s hard to shake off the feeling that no one really sees how tough this is, and that makes it even heavier.

automatic_thoughts: will it ever really get better?
no one really sees how tough this is

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight right now. When you think about whether things will get better, what evidence do you have that they might? Are there moments, however small, where you notice a shift or a bit of relief?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel really heavy. I mean, there have been moments, like when I managed to connect with a couple of colleagues who get it. We shared some laughs, and it felt nice to feel a little lighter, even if just for a bit. But then I’m back to this nagging thought that it’s just temporary, and I wonder if I’m just fooling myself into thinking things could actually change. I guess it’s hard to hold onto those small wins when the bigger

patient.query: I don’t know… I mean, the moments with my colleagues sometimes feel like a glimpse of what it could be, but then I just slide back into that feeling of being stuck. It’s like I can’t really trust those good feelings will stick around, you know? What if it’s just me convincing myself it’s okay for a second, and then I crash again? patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do worry about that. I don't know if I have real evidence, just a nagging voice that tells me it won't last. But there have definitely been times when I’ve had good days that felt a bit brighter—like after a successful meeting or when I got some praise from my boss. Those moments felt nice, but then they seem to fade away so quickly, and I’m left with this weight again. It’s like I can’t fully enjoy them because I’m

patient.query: Yeah, I see what you mean, but it just feels like those good moments always end up overshadowed by something that goes wrong. It’s frustrating. I mean, I can think of a few times where something really made me feel good, like when I received that compliment from my boss. But even that felt like a setup for the next disappointment. I keep waiting for the other shoe to drop, and it makes it hard to really savor those good experiences. I guess I just feel afraid that

patient.query: I keep waiting for the other shoe to drop, and it makes it hard to really savor those good experiences. Sometimes I think about how those moments that felt good always seem to lead to something else going wrong. Like, I can remember when my boss praised me, and it felt nice, but then I ended up making a mistake right after that, and it just overshadowed everything. It’s like I’m afraid to believe that things can go well because I’ve been let down so often.
